column3: Dottorando presso Università degli Studi di Padova
column7:
I've just released the prototype of my ifcSQL_Tools for QGIS!For anyone dealing with BIM and GIS interoperability, the plugin allows you to interact with IFC data stored in the ifcSQL database, directly within the QGIS environment.Key features include:- Load all your georeferenced IFC models and view them simultaneously on a map (in 2D or 3D)- Filter and display only the IFC classes that interest you- Delete the IFC models you no longer need from the databaseIn the attached GIF, you can see an example of a complete WWTP (Wastewater Treatment Plant) made up of 21 different IFC files, successfully imported and navigated in 3D.You can find the public repository along with the installation instructions here: https://lnkd.in/eUU-zkExThe tool is currently in the development phase. I invite you to test it out and share your feedback.This research work has been carried out during my PhD at University of Padova (Italy) together with multiutility company ETRA S.p.A. Società benefit. The implementation part has been carried out mainly during my stay at TU Delft as visiting PhD.I would like to thank: Prof. Carlo Zanchetta (University of Padova), Igino Taverna (ETRA S.p.A. Società benefit), Dr. Giorgio Agugiaro and Dr. Ken Arroyo Ohori (TU Delft). #BIMGIS #QGIS #IFC #ifcSQL #OpenBIM

Cities are full of it:Sensor networks. Mobility flows. Land use patterns. Demographic layers.The problem isn't the data.The problem is that most smart city frameworks are built on „average city“ models. It optimize for efficiency and scale horizontally. But cities are vertical in complexity — every neighborhood, every migration pattern, every system is different.What's missing is personalization at the territorial level — tools that treat cities as living, context-specific systems, not templates to be replicated.That's the gap SIM by lTS builds.Image DesktopSetUp  By Author:What you're seeing on screen:→ Two interfaces in active development + slice of the backend that connects them.Built from scratch. column3: Research software engineer building climate data tools and open science infrastructure
column7:
I just rebuilt our entire climate data catalog from scratch, and made a cool web app to search and download from it! 😄 Link in the comments 👇  Everyone close to me knows I love my job, in part because I get a lot of space to learn and explore in my role. This past month, I went down a bit of a rabbit hole 😅 learning about STAC API. I ended up rebuilding our entire catalog— bulking it up to include all of Cal-Adapt’s 11 publicly available data collections, adding a bunch of cool new features & metadata, and learning a ton about database management and AWS along the way. And, I coded up a user-friendly web app that lets you search and download data from our catalog (built on top of Radiant Earth's awesome STAC Browser!).With this web app, you can see so much info about all our data that was previously pretty hard to access and search through, including: - metadata: scientific DOIs, licenses, geometry files 🌍 - the ability to query/filter data based on different features 💻  - easy download button for tabular and netcdf data 👇  - cute little animated icons for each dataset 🕺  - auto-generated code for each dataset that links to our custom open source python library for climate analysisAnd, I got to build it using open source tools, and make the final products open source as well! Yay! Would love to hear what you think!

column3: Earth → Data → Insight | Co-Founder, Terra Helix
column7:
So, I made a thing: https://mapsum.devI was prototyping a map-based app and needed a placeholder for the map container. Not a grey box. An actual map. Turns out nobody has built a placeholder image service for maps. If you want one, you either wire up a mapping library or screenshot something and crop it.So I built Lorem Mapsum, which is a terrible name and I love it.You construct a URL with the dimensions you want and you get back a map image. You don't need an API key or an account. The maps are rendered from OpenStreetMap data. I downloaded city extracts via Osmium, turned them into vector tiles with Planetiler, and rendered master images through MapLibre and Puppeteer. The API crops and resizes on demand, and Cloudflare caches everything at the edge.I built most of it with Claude Code. I've been a bit averse towards vibe coding so far, so this was a good exercise to understand Claude Code and its complexities. It solves a small problem. I hope someone else finds it useful.

column3: Senior Geospatial Developer | Spatial Data Engineering | Automation
column7:
Designing an OSM-Based Accessibility Engine for Parcel-Level AnalysisIn many geospatial workflows, parcel accessibility is treated as a binary condition. Either a property is considered accessible due to proximity to a road, or it is not. In practice, this simplification breaks down quickly. Accessibility depends on multiple factors, including road classification, frontage, routing feasibility, and the reliability of underlying data sources.To address this, I built an accessibility engine using OpenStreetMap (OSM), Overpass API, and OSRM, combined with a spatial processing layer using Shapely and PyProj. The system evaluates parcels by first extracting nearby road geometries, filtering them based on public accessibility criteria, and computing spatial relationships to parcel boundaries. It then attempts to validate real-world reachability through routing, rather than relying solely on geometric proximity.A key design decision was to separate spatial computation from interpretation. Deterministic metrics, such as frontage length, minimum distance to road, and routing success, are computed first and normalized into a structured schema. From there, the system derives higher-level signals (e.g., physical access strength, frontage confidence, routing confirmation) and aggregates them into a scoring model. This approach avoids over-reliance on any single data source and provides a more realistic representation of access conditions.The system also accounts for real-world failure modes. External providers such as Overpass or routing services may fail or return incomplete data, so fallback logic and confidence degradation are built into the pipeline. Rather than forcing a binary outcome, the system surfaces uncertainty explicitly, allowing downstream consumers to make informed decisions.This type of layered approach, combining spatial geometry, network validation, and structured interpretation can serve as a foundation for broader geospatial decision systems, including land feasibility analysis, development screening, and automated underwriting workflows.#Geospatial #GIS #GeospatialEngineering #SpatialData #WebGIS #DataEngineering #SystemDesign #OpenStreetMap #SpatialAnalysis#PropTech #RealEstateTechnology #AppliedAI

column3: 🌍 Merging AI & Geospatial Tech
column7:
From 3D City Model → Photorealistic Urban Scene.This is Generative GIS.By integrating diffusion models with GIS, we can move beyond traditional analysis into what I’d call Generative GIS.I built a ComfyUI pipeline that uses 3D GIS massing models as a structural blueprint for Stable Diffusion. It’s an alternative to traditional rendering engines, moving from explicit ray-tracing to neural synthesis.⚙️ The Pipeline:🔷 Input: ArcGIS 3D city massing (footprints + heights)🔷 Structural Guidance: Metric3D (Depth/Normals) + M-LSD Lines🔷 Control: ControlNet Union SDXL (keeping the geometry "locked")🔷 Inference: JuggernautXL v9 + Ultimate SD UpscaleThe idea is simple:Use the geometric intelligence inside a 3D GIS model as structural guidance, and let Stable Diffusion render a photorealistic world on top of it.When geospatial data becomes the blueprint and diffusion models become the renderer, urban visualization starts to change fundamentally.Happy to share the full ComfyUI workflow for anyone exploring this space.#DigitalTwin #GeospatialAI #SmartCities #ArchitectureAI #AIVisualization #GIS #GenerativeAI #StableDiffusion #UrbanPlanning #GeoAI #ComfyUI #0to100xengineer

column3: assistant professor | cities | earth observation | maps
column7:
📢 New Publication: Open data-based urban models: an assessment for Canadian cities With Matthew Glowacki, Zach Pankratz, Reece Cullen, Ben Boswick, and Kyra KwiatkowskiFull paper is freely accessible via: https://lnkd.in/gQZgtZmJ Short research summary: https://lnkd.in/gryJ9m2z To democratize access to advanced urban models, this paper investigates the feasibility of utilizing Open Data-Based Urban Models (ODUMs) in Canada. It provides a comprehensive evaluation of municipal open data infrastructure across Canada and demonstrates its practical application at the City of Winnipeg. We developed an assessment framework to compare the open data portals of 11 North American cities, including Toronto, Montreal, Vancouver, and Winnipeg, and 3 international benchmark cities, namely Helsinki, Singapore, and Zurich. The open datasets were evaluated for timeliness, accessibility, and metadata quality to assess their readiness for GIS-based modelling. The assessment revealed significant cross-city variability and highlighted distinct gaps in Canada’s open data infrastructure. While vegetation and transportation datasets are consistently available and high-scoring across Canadian portals, critical layers such as buildings, energy, and socio-economic data are frequently absent or unevenly published as open datasets to researchers and the general public. This research establishes a replicable methodology for resource-constrained municipalities to build their own urban digital twins. Ultimately, we advocate establishing a national open data standard in Canada to improve data accessibility, enhance interoperability among cities, and further empower data-driven urban planning and community engagement.Many thanks the University of Manitoba’s University Research Grants Program (Early Career Stream) and the Faculty of Architecture for funding (research start-up funding for Y.L.) for this research. We also appreciate the valuable comments and suggestions from the editors at the Canadian Geographies and two anonymous reviewers.

column2: Isaac Xiao🇸🇬 PMP®, CSM®, MSCS, ICP-ACC®, ICP-ATF®
column3: PMP®, Certified ScrumMaster® (CSM®), MSCS, ICP-ACC®, ICP-ATF®, MIES
column7:
I am excited to share a visualization from a recent urban graph learning pipeline built with GeoPandas, NetworkX, and PyTorch Geometric.This graph represents Singapore's urban network using 478 planning zones and 5,574 bus stops. It includes 2,390 proximity edges connecting bus stops to nearby zones and 8,005 contiguity edges representing zone-to-zone adjacency, for a total of 10,395 edges.This hybrid structure captures both spatial proximity and administrative contiguity, which is a powerful foundation for urban mobility modeling, accessibility analysis, and graph-based transport forecasting.Graph representations like this help answer critical questions such as which zones are underserved by bus stops, how contiguity affects route planning, and where new stops should be added to maximize coverage.The pipeline was built with open tools and data from OpenStreetMap contributors, GeoPandas for spatial operations, NetworkX for graph construction, and PyTorch Geometric for downstream learning tasks.This is part of a #city2graph approach that turns cities into learnable graphs. #GraphNeuralNetworks #UrbanAnalytics #GeoPandas #PyTorchGeometric #Singapore #SmartCity #TransportModeling #NetworkX #OpenStreetMap

column3: Research Fellow at the National University of Singapore
column7:
How do urban planning regulations shape the performance of distributed solar energy systems?Our new article, “An integrated approach for assessing the impact of land-use regulations on distributed solar energy system performance”, which addresses the question, is now published in Sustainable Cities and Society. This paper:1) proposed an integrated framework that links land use regulations with energy system optimization;2) quantified the impact of LUR on distributed solar energy system performance;3) identified essential planning parameters that shape DSES performance outcomes.The methodology is demonstrated in the One-North district in Singapore. The marginal effect analysis revealed that DSES's performance was sensitive to allowable GFAs and relaxation for certain land use types, especially in mixed-use zones. Our findings underscore our efforts in urban form and urban performance, and continue to highlight the interconnection of urban performance and computational urban design towards sustainable urban planning.Read the paper: https://lnkd.in/gXuWRyrUAs a co-first author on this project with Jidong Kang, I'd like to thank our brilliant co-authors, Christoph Waibel, Pieter Herthogs, and Yuming Fu.  Grateful for the collaboration within the Future Cities Laboratory Global, Singapore-ETH Centre, and NUS Department of Architecture, NUS College of Design and Engineering. #Sustainability #Solarenergy #ComputationalUrbanDesign #SmartCities

column3: Founder at PlanFinder
column7:
One thing I keep coming back to in floor plan generation:the hard part is not only generating plans, but deciding how to represent them in the first place.I’ve had clients for whom only a procedural representation was really viable. In one warehouse racking project, a code-based approach was necessary to handle the constraints properly; without it, you quickly end up with sub-optimal solutions.In other cases, grids work well because they fit vision-based models. Graphs can capture structure compactly. Polygons are often more natural when reasoning directly over rooms themselves.There are of course other possibilities too: sequence-based, constraint-based, hybrid, but these four come up a lot.There probably isn’t one best representation, only better fits for specific tasks.

column3: Prof. at Università del Piemonte Orientale
column7:
🏙️ Is the "15-Minute City" truly inclusive, or are we masking urban segregation?I am honored to share our latest research published in PLOS ONE: "Understanding the interplay between urban segregation and accessibility to services with network analysis".While the "15-minute city" model, and chrono-urbanism in general, is designed to improve urban livability and sustainability by placing essential services within a short walk or ride from home, our study asks if this hyper-proximity model might inadvertently reinforce spatial segregation. Our Network-Based Approach: We developed a multi-dimensional framework to analyze 81 cities worldwide, quantifying accessibility through Points of Interest (PoIs) and connectivity using closeness centrality. This analysis was conducted at multiple scales, from individual residential addresses (street intersections) to neighborhood clusters detected by the Infomap algorithm. The Accessibility vs. Connectivity Paradox: Our findings show that high accessibility does not always mean high connectivity to the rest of the city.Paris: A leader in accessibility with 99.7% of residents living within 15 minutes of services, yet it ranks 71st for connectivity, indicating potential "bubbles" of local access that are disconnected from the broader urban network.Rio de Janeiro: Conversely, this city shows high connectivity while ranking significantly lower in local service accessibility.Fine-Grained Insights & Spatial Justice: By analyzing districts, census areas, and street intersections, we identified that these patterns are highly heterogeneous.The Double Burden: Many lower-income neighborhoods suffer from both low accessibility and weak connectivity, a finding particularly evident in our case study of Italian cities.Voluntary Isolation: In wealthy districts of cities like Naples, Bologna, and Turin, we observed high-income residents living in areas with low accessibility and connectivity, suggesting patterns of "voluntary isolation" and a continued reliance on private transport.Our research highlights that hyper-proximity alone does not guarantee inclusiveness and may mask underlying socio-economic inequalities. For planners, our framework provides some pointers for corrective actions to ensure spatial justice for all residents. 🙏 Huge thanks to the rest of the team: Mirko Lai, Anna Sapienza, Salvatore Vilella, Massimo Canonico, and Federica Cena. Kudos to filippo barbera, the first person that introduced me to the "15 minute city" idea some years ago. 📖 Read the full open-access paper: https://lnkd.in/dcCHHKVa 💻 Explore our data & code: https://lnkd.in/dDaxPyWU#UrbanPlanning #15MinuteCity #SpatialJustice #DataScience #NetworkAnalysis #NetworkScience #Sustainability #UrbanSegregation #PLOSONE #SmartCities #UrbanAnalytics

column3: Founder at Urban Col.labs - Placemaking powered by data
column7:
I am very happy to share Urban Col.labs first paper in a three-part publication: Beyond Mapping - Location Intelligence & Site OptimisationIn this paper, we analyse the locations shortlisted by the UK New Towns Taskforce, commissioned by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government.Download the full paper here: https://lnkd.in/dw_4kQXR Taking a people-first lens; affordability, deprivation (IoD25) and social housing need, to highlight where pressure concentrates and what “early-phase” moves can turn need into opportunity. Showcasing how early stage, in depth spatial data analysis can contribute to more robust decision making and place optimization.This approach reflects how we apply design-led, data-informed thinking to support more effective site strategies across diverse contexts.Key takeaways from our research:-Several areas with high demand for affordable and social housing remain over an hour from the proposed locations, highlighting opportunities for additional development and improved transport connectivity to bridge this gap.-The proposed locations reflect two distinct starting conditions—some in low-deprivation areas and others in high-deprivation contexts—implying different risk profiles and early priorities across sites.-A new town succeeds when it makes everyday life easier, safer, and more accessible for everyone.The publication forms part of a wider research in collaboration with Okana and Ryder Architecture: A diverse approach to the UK housing challenge. Two further parts of this series will follow, focusing on Place and Performance.#UrbanPlanning #HousingCrisis #NewTowns #SpatialData #GIS #CityPlanning #UrbanDevelopment #PlaceMaking #BuiltEnvironment #DataDriven #HousingPolicy #UKHousing #SustainableCities #UrbanStrategy #LocationIntelligence #UrbanCollabs

column3: Design Technology | Computational Design | Workflow Products | Construction Tech
column7:
Lately I’ve been exploring something that sits slightly outside the usual use of Grasshopper.Not geometry.Not architecture.But data from completely different domains.I came across components that allow working with atomic and molecular structures, and it raised a simple question:Why is there chemistry inside a design tool?After looking into it, the answer seems more interesting than expected.Grasshopper was never only about geometry.It is essentially a data processing environment where geometry happens to be one of the outputs.In these experiments, molecular datasets such as DNA fragments or proteins (like 1BNA, 1CRN or hemoglobin structures) are translated into point networks, relationships and visual states.What is striking is not the result itself, but the similarity in logic:• Elements become objects• Attributes become data• Bonds become relationships• Structures become systemsThe same way we handle architectural models. At some point, the boundary between disciplines starts to disappear.Grasshopper becomes less of a modeling tool and more of a platform to structure and visualize complex datasets.Which raises a broader question:How many other fields are already using these tools beyond geometry?And more importantly:Are we underestimating what these environments are actually capable of?#ComputationalDesign #DesignTechnology #DataDrivenDesign #DataVisualization

column3: Associate Professor Land use and Urban development
column7:
Can we map slums with high spatial detail using only publicly available data?Earlier studies have shown that we can accurately map slums using very-high resolution satellite imagery. Yet, this approach is not scalable due to the high costs for imagery, limiting their application at scale. Additionally, the computational resources required for this approach are significant, further constraining their scalability.In our new paper, we use morphological characteristics derived from publicly available building-footprint datasets to classify slum and non-slum areas at a building level. The use of building footprints allows to derive relevant metrics, such as size, distance to neighbors, and irregularity directly, so there is no need for a deep-learning approach to derive such spatial patterns indirectly from satellite imagery.The model reaches an F1 score of 0.987 in a K-means cross-validation. More importantly, our approach achieves an F1-score of 0.742 on cities that are completely unseen in the training process, indicating its transferability across cities.The analyses (we tested a lot of different models) was done by Hang Yang, and we had great help on interpreting the Kenyan context from Eunice Jimmy Ph.D! Additional thanks to Monika Kuffer, Peter Verburg, and Alex Levering for their valuable contributions!The image shows the share of buildings identified as a slum at a 100-meter resolution for four cities in Kenya.Link to the paper: https://lnkd.in/eeN4-Vck

column3: Design, Technology, Planet
column7:
We just open-sourced 36 AI skills for architects. Here's how it works.I see Anthropic 's Claude Code as very similar to Grasshopper. It opens the door for deep experts to package knowledge and logic in ways that are highly shareable and reusable across their firms. Grasshopper did it for computational design. "Skills" for Claude Code do this for workflow automation like zoning rules, spec conventions, product research, site analysis all encoded as small skills that anyone in your studio can install and use. We've been testing this idea for the past few months with my friend Mick McConnell. Then I tested it with rebuilding some of Canoa 's features for a friend. Then again with other friends at a small firm that just needed the help. We started building skills one at a time. A skill for zoning analysis. One for EPD parsing. One for product research. Eventually we started tying them together with agents that orchestrate these skills and rules that govern the output. Before we knew it we had a pretty expansive set of tasks and wanted to see how far we could take it. How many tasks can be automated this way? The short answer is, a lot.Today we're open-sourcing the result.The open source Architecture Studio project is a plugin for Claude Code that runs in your terminal. It gives Claude architecture-specific rules, packages common tasks into skills, and routes requests through specialist agents.Type /studio and describe what you need. The router figures out the rest.A few of the agents:- Site Planner - give it an address, it researches climate, flood zones, seismic risk, transit, demographics, and neighborhood history, then synthesizes a site brief. For a recent project, work that usually takes a day of pulling from NOAA, FEMA, USGS, and Census came back in a few minutes.- Product & Materials Researcher - give it a brief or a rep's PDF, it extracts specs, tags products by category and material, finds alternatives, and writes everything to a shared Google Sheet.- Sustainability Specialist - give it your materials, it finds EPDs from EC3, compares embodied carbon side by side, and checks LEED eligibility.Every output follows a transparency rule: every number links to its public source. Every calculation shows the inputs and the formula. If we reference a building code, you get a link to the government-published version. Our view is that if you can't see the math, the tool is hiding something.It's all MIT licensed and actively growing - very New York-centric on zoning and due diligence right now, but the architecture works for any jurisdiction.Try it if you feel like it. Fork it if you want to make it yours. The contributing guide is in the repo. The link to the repo is in the comments below.#architecture #opensource #AEC #claudecode #workplacedesign #sustainabledesign #interiordesign

column3: Architect | Computational Design Expert | Full Stack Developer
column7:
🪶 Turn Rhino into a lightweight BIM system.Most Rhino / Grasshopper workflows lose all data the moment geometry is baked. This one doesn’t.In this demo, I’m taking a data-rich model and baking it into Rhino blocks using the Elefront plugin for Grasshopper.Not just geometry.Full key–value data included.Which means you can:• Keep designing in Rhino• While still working with structured data• And query everything using Elefront filtersFilter by layer.Filter by property.Extract schedules.Drive decisions.All after baking.Important note:In this example I’m starting from a Speckle model.But you don’t necessarily have to (they work nicely together though 🙂).You could just as well define Elefront blocks + properties directly inside Grasshopper. Same principle.Elefront is often used to bake geometry and manage blocks.But if you treat those blocks as data containers, it becomes something much more powerful.We often treat parametric design and BIM as two separate worlds:Parametric → flexible, fast, but “dumb” geometryBIM → structured, but heavy and rigid.But workflows like this start to blur that line.You get something closer to a lightweight BIM system inside Rhino:Geometry + dataFlexible + structuredI feel like most teams are still treating Rhino models as disposable geometry.Which is a missed opportunity.Curious how others are handling this 👇How do you deal with data once geometry is baked?• Keep it alive in Rhino?• Move to BIM tools?• Or rebuild it later?Shoutout to Ramon van der Heijden for creating this plugin!

🚀 Simulador Estratégico de Ciudad – Fase I: Visor Diagnóstico -> ✅Si hace un par de meses se alcanzaba el primer hito del PEM, publicando el DIE y el BASE (https://lnkd.in/eEkWpCtG), hoy hemos dado un paso clave en la transformación urbana de Madrid con la presentación de la Fase I del Simulador Estratégico de Ciudad - el Visor Diagnóstico, una herramienta desarrollada en solo 6 meses que permite conocer el estado de la ciudad.Esta primera versión sienta las bases del Plan Estratégico Municipal, el primer plan nativo digital de la ciudad, con la integración de más de 190 indicadores, 134 conjuntos de datos procedentes de 37 fuentes oficiales, que ofrece una visión conjunta y multiescalar del territorio: desde el municipio hasta la parcela.📊 Tecnología al servicio del interés general, mejorando la toma de decisiones públicas y la detección de desequilibrios urbanos.Nada de esto habría sido posible sin el trabajo conjunto dentro del Ayuntamiento de Madrid —Iam Informática del Ayuntamiento de Madrid Juan Corro y la Oficina Digital del Ayuntamiento de Madrid  Fernando de Pablo Martín — y la colaboración público-privada que tenemos en marcha!¡Gracias de verdad a todos los que estáis haciendo posible este cambio de paradigma!
